icpBlurb: FDA-regulated handheld ultrasound: design history files are mandatory, so traceable reviews are a regulatory requirement, not a nice-to-have.
snapshot: Builds handheld whole-body ultrasound probes using proprietary Ultrasound-on-Chip semiconductor tech.
Ships FDA-cleared iQ3/iQ+ devices plus AI software to 145,000+ global customers.

teamSignal.label: Open roles across hardware, firmware, and clinical engineering

compliance.note: Devices explicitly described as FDA-cleared for external ultrasound imaging.
Standard applies given embedded medical device software (AI-driven imaging, Compass AI) though not explicitly named in text.

toolchain.sourceQuote: We put ultrasound on a chip and created the world's first handheld whole-body imager. — implies in-house chip design requiring ASIC/semiconductor design tools.
Butterfly's Ultrasound-on-Chip technology... connects to a compatible iPhone or Android smartphone — implies firmware bridging custom silicon to mobile software.

hypotheses.title: Fragile audit trails for FDA-regulated firmware changes
hypotheses.evidence: Butterfly's iQ3/iQ+ are explicitly FDA-cleared medical devices with embedded AI software (Compass AI), which falls under IEC 62304 traceability requirements. Without a version-controlled, sign-off-driven review process, proving design change history for FDA audits becomes a manual, error-prone burden.

hypotheses.title: Costly re-spins on proprietary Ultrasound-on-Chip ASIC
hypotheses.evidence: The company states 'We put ultrasound on a chip and created the world's first handheld whole-body imager,' implying custom semiconductor design where a layout error is extremely expensive to fix post-tapeout. Visual diffing across schematic/layout revisions would catch these issues before an ASIC re-spin is committed.

hypotheses.title: Disjointed hardware-firmware-clinical handoffs
hypotheses.evidence: Butterfly's careers page lists open roles spanning hardware, firmware, and clinical engineering, and their product connects custom silicon to iPhone/Android software (iQ3, iQ+, Bladder, Vet, Move variants). Coordinating design reviews and sign-offs across these disciplines and product lines without a PR-style workflow risks miscommunication on safety-critical changes.

questions.text: How do hardware, firmware, and clinical teams coordinate on design reviews today?
Walk me through what happens when a schematic or layout error surfaces late.
How many ASIC re-spins have you had this year, and what caused them?
What happens when a firmware update affects an already-cleared hardware revision?
How do you currently track and prove design change history for FDA audits?
How much engineering time weekly goes into manual sign-off tracking or audit prep?

storyline.step: Visual diff of chip schematic revision
Pull request review on firmware change
